content:
5 min read
7 hours ago

--

I tried to build the ‘perfect’ Angular codebase using every clean code principle I knew. Two years and ten major versions later, reality forced a re-think it a bit. At first it was easy, but the more code we had, the more maintenance we had. We started in 2022, and each Angular update meant a new change or refactor. Only when we changed styles in Angular Material and they changed the internal CSS did we have to refactor everything. And this maintenance because of updates took us a lot of effort and sometimes compromise.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Gemini generatedWhat Is The Issue?
I am not talking about generic advice such as: read Clean Code, nor about Angular Architecture. But what are the issues you can have in your app when it gets bigger?
Even if you are a senior developer, you are still learning. For example, I did a custom tooltip 4 years ago because I was in a hurry and I did it quite wrong. I had a service, a directive, and a component for the tooltip itself — even though I needed only a directive. But once we removed zone.js and started to be dependent on signals, I had to refactor that component. With more knowledge and the new Angular, the new version was much more simple — but the realization of making it from scratch and not refactoring the old version wasn’t easy to see at first sight!
The Initial Idea
I wanted to keep it simple: to have a max. of 250–300 lines per component, and to use composition as much as possible. I wanted to have one source of truth for the data. I wanted to be up to date and have a reasonable amount of dependencies. And I didn’t want to compromise performance. I wanted to keep a clear distance between parent and dummy components etc., and also to have a system in my CSS files.
What Have We Compromised?
We started with Angular 12 now we have 22. Our MVP grew relatively quickly and I was happy about it. But then the Angular Material change came -> it was basically 1 month of component refactors due to our CSS rewrites -> they started to use CSS variables, which is great.
After one month of refactoring, we were a bit late in regards to the features delivered. So I was in a hurry and did not respect parent-dummy components and put some logic into components instead of facades. We don’t have 80% test coverage, but we are closer to 20% (but we are doing our happy path and business logic).
What Can We Not Compromise?
We have realized that a bit of mess is still ok until you respect readable methods and try to do as small methods as possible -> like that you can easily copy-paste them through your folder structure(copy paste them to put them on different place if needed, not to create duplication)
Also, regular updates are very important. Because the later you update, the more refactoring you will need to handle.

An example of a not best-handled situation, but a business-related comment saved that piece of code (that’s why it is a good idea to add from time to time some business-related comments
Press enter or click to view image in full size

An example of a situation that wasn’t handled best, but a business-related comment saved that piece of code. That’s why it is a good idea to add business-related comments from time to time — of course, do not explain the code, but explain the ‘whys’.What Makes It Ok To Have Some Tech Debt — and Some Legacy Code in a Startup?
Well imagine, you create some feature for 1–5% of use cases, but it is needed and you cannot remove it. Then this piece of code becomes very costly to maintain. So we analysed such places and we decided that we will maintain them only in a way with library updates etc., but we are leaving the code as it is. It is quicker to redo it from scratch in the future than to maintain it.
From a User’s Perspective?
The user only cares about what he does often with your app — a happy path and performance, if it loads fast and if the app doesn’t make him angry. On the other hand, DX is something different. The user doesn’t care if you have 4 levels of inheritance, or a 1,000-line component (your future self cares about it, though).
How To Scale and Maintain a Large Codebase With This Knowledge?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Reused logic — used in your views: should be in facadesThe reality showed and double-confirmed the clean code guidelines.
Try to have as small methods as possible

• Then it is easy to refactor or change.
Take care of naming

• That way you can easily navigate your app and codebase and quickly get what piece of code belongs to which logic, as this is the operation that you will do the most — looking for something.
3-second rule

• The piece of code you care about or functionality should take you 3 seconds to understand — if it takes more, it means that you should rename it, shorten the methods, or add a business-related comment.
Update your packages

• You should regularly update your npm dependencies; even better, use a tool such as Renovate.
Try to avoid dependencies

• Do you need a time library? You can use the new ECMAScript. Do you need your component library? Wouldn’t it be better to use Angular CDK and create your own components? (With AI it is quite quick and you won’t be dependent on external library changes).
Have a strict linter and Prettier

• It prevents your future lazy self.
Each piece of logic you are not sure will stay:

• Such logic has to be strictly written in a clean code standard: so have your private methods with return types, and name them — in the future it will be easy to move them across your codebase.
How to work with AI?
In a larger app, I do not recommend vibe coding. I recommend doing something similar to test-driven development.
Firstly, on paper, write what you need and write your architecture. Then create the files with classes and the names of the methods. Then write some of the methods by yourself and others with AI when they have the return type. Like that, you will be more productive but you will own 100% of the code.
For example:
export class MyTodos {
public todos: TODO[] = []; addTodo(todo: TODO): TODO[] {
// AI can implement this
}
removeTodo(id: string): TODO[] {
// AI can implement this
}
}
This code now can be enhanced by AI and also unit tested, but you are the brain of the code.
Conclusion
Striving for perfection can make you crazy or you might not finish the project. As in software development, we have to challenge constant change, especially with a lot of new updates that cause us to refactor some code already written — which stops you from developing new features but puts you in maintenance mode.
